ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton entry; no specific shrimp-pork wonton with peanut sesame sauce entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for shrimp-pork wontons in peanut sesame sauce. A0CKP (filled pasta / dumpling category) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for this dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of shrimp and pork wontons in peanut sesame sauce is approximately 200 g, equivalent to about 10 wontons, as commonly served in Singaporean Chinese restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp & Pork Wantons In Peanut Sesame Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 麻酱虾肉馄饨 Shrimp & Pork Wantons In Peanut Sesame Sauce (cr_id G00010055). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this entry. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous shrimp-pork dumpling entry (USDA SR Legacy #35196 and #10006 composite) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on boiled wheat-based dumplings / wontons
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shrimp-pork wontons in peanut sesame sauce. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on boiled wheat-flour dumplings / wontons from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 28–55 for boiled wheat dumplings). The peanut-sesame sauce contributes fat and protein that further attenuate postprandial glucose response. Carbohydrate per 100g is recorded as 0g in the HPB source, which likely reflects the filling only; wonton wrappers contribute refined carbohydrate not fully captured. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100; because HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0g, GL calculates to 0.0 — this is a data artefact of the HPB entry and is noted accordingly.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; potassium at only 47 mg/100 g provides negligible offsetting effect
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request sauce on the side, limit portion to no more than 100 g, and compensate with high-potassium foods such as bananas or leafy greens at the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0g/100g but wonton wrappers contribute refined wheat carbohydrate not fully captured; high protein (19.1 g/100g) and fat (10.5 g/100g) slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should be aware that wonton wrappers add refined carbohydrate not reflected in this nutrient record; limit portion to 100–150 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response, particularly given the high sodium content which may exacerbate insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100 g poses significant blood-pressure burden; cholesterol at 89 mg/100 g and estimated saturated fat contribute to atherogenic risk; pork filling adds arachidonic acid (omega-6) which may amplify inflammatory pathways
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food only; request reduced-sodium sauce, limit portion to 100 g or less, and balance the meal with omega-3-rich foods to offset the pro-inflammatory omega-6 load from pork.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, worsening tissue oedema; pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid this dish; if consumed, request reduced-sodium sauce and consider substituting pork with chicken or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shrimp and Pork Wontons in Peanut Sesame Sauce (麻酱虾肉馄饨) is a Chinese-origin dish popular in Singapore's hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. Thin wheat-flour wonton wrappers are filled with a mixture of minced shrimp and pork, then boiled until tender and tossed in a bold, nutty peanut-sesame sauce (麻酱, má jiàng) that is typically made from sesame paste, peanut butter, sesame oil, soy sauce, garlic, and chilli. The result is a rich, savoury, slightly spicy dish with a silky, chewy texture from the wonton skin and a juicy, protein-dense filling. Per 100 g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and a notably high sodium content of 1050 mg, making it a high-protein but high-sodium food. The HPB nutrient record reports 0 g carbohydrate, which reflects the filling composition; the wonton wrappers contribute refined wheat carbohydrate not fully captured in this entry. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its very high sodium load. For people with type 2 diabetes, the high protein and fat content attenuate glycaemic response, but portion control is advised given the unrecorded wrapper carbohydrate. The peanut-sesame sauce is a source of healthy unsaturated fats but also adds caloric density. This dish is not halal due to pork content and contains major allergens including crustaceans, gluten, peanuts, sesame, and soy.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in shrimp pork wontons?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010055, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entries and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat dumplings, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this entry. This is likely a data artefact reflecting the filling composition only; wonton wrappers contribute refined wheat carbohydrate not fully captured in the HPB record. The value is preserved as reported by the approved source (HPB) and flagged here. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for this entry. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for this entry. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010055, and no sufficiently close analogue with complete micronutrient data was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for shrimp (#15152) and ground pork (#10006) in proportions consistent with a typical wonton filling, combined with peanut-sesame sauce fat contribution; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. 4. GI NOTE: The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0g/100g causes glycemic_load_per_serving to calculate as 0.0; this is a data artefact. The actual GL for a 200g serving would be approximately 4–9 if wrapper carbohydrate (~10–20g per serving) is included. 5. HALAL STATUS: This dish contains pork and is therefore not halal.
